guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology — Psoriasis (topical corticosteroids, emollients, when to refer)
Australian Family Physician / DermNet NZ — Plaque psoriasis and psoriasis flare management
DermNet NZ — Erythrodermic psoriasis and generalised pustular psoriasis (dermatological emergencies)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: an acute flare of a KNOWN 10-year chronic condition presented alarmingly (severe skin pain, continuous bleeding from cracked plaques, temporary immobility, an out-of-hours 'urgent' triage framing that explicitly invokes erythroderma and sepsis). The source note's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (erythema <90% BSA so NOT erythrodermic, no high fever or rigors, stable vitals, no systemic toxicity) and its in-person disposition is routine (topical treatment + urgent dermatology referral + GP review in 3-5 days). The correct telehealth answer is to recognise a severe-but-benign plaque flare, tier it T2, give flare management, and attach PROMINENT MANDATORY safety-netting to T4/T5 for the serious mimics. Escalating this stable flare to ED/000 on the strength of the pain and the out-of-hours framing is the primary scored over-triage defect.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who elicited the Auspitz sign by lifting a scale, palpated fissures, assessed nail pitting, estimated body-surface-area involvement, and physically excluded erythroderma / pustular psoriasis / secondary sepsis. Breath-Ezy cannot examine, scrape, palpate or measure clinician vitals; those confirmatory and exclusionary findings are sealed. The AI is scored on recognising a psoriasis flare from history plus video-observable appearance, and on giving safe topical + emollient + analgesia advice with the right disposition and safety-net.

Important-not-to-miss mimic screen kept honest: erythrodermic psoriasis, generalised pustular psoriasis, secondary staphylococcal infection/sepsis and eczema herpeticum are the dangerous look-alikes/complications the in-person clinician excluded. The AI cannot examine to exclude them definitively, so the correct disposition builds them into an explicit, mandatory safety-net (whole-body redness / high fever + rigors / spreading pustules / purulent weeping / inability to keep warm) rather than a reflexive urgent escalation of the current stable flare.
Recent streptococcal throat infection and severe psychosocial stress are named as the flare triggers — relevant history the AI should elicit but which do not themselves raise acuity.
Age (~48, adult) and sex_at_birth (male) inferred — no age or sex is stated in the source note; flagged for clinician confirmation. No paediatric dosing involved.
Source category: DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue), source note DST-033.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
